TMJ Health Depends on Proper Implant Function The temporomandibular joint (TMJ) connects your jawbone to your skull.

When one tooth is replaced, it affects: The adjacent teeth The opposing tooth in the opposite arch The way you bite, chew, and speak Without proper planning, an implant can end up disrupting this balance, leading to: Bite misalignment Excess pressure on surrounding teeth Muscle strain or jaw pain Occlusion (Your Bite) Must Be Considered Occlusion refers to how your teeth meet when you close your mouth.

If your bite is off—even slightly—it can place undue stress on the TMJ, leading to: Clicking or popping sounds Headaches and neck pain Jaw fatigue or difficulty opening/closing the mouth Long-term joint damage That’s why our planning includes jaw alignment and function—to ensure your new teeth support a healthy, relaxed bite.

For patients who feel anxious about surgery or are concerned about complications, guided implantology brings clarity, control, and confidence to every step. What is Guided Dental Implant Surgery. Guided implant surgery is a technique where the entire procedure is digitally planned before surgery. Using 3D CBCT scans and digital impressions, we create a virtual model of your mouth to determine the exact position, angle, and depth of the implant placement.

From this data, we fabricate a custom surgical guide, a template that fits perfectly in your mouth, ensuring the implant is precisely placed. Benefits of Guided Implants Unmatched Accuracy The digital guide ensures the implant is placed in the pre-planned location, avoiding nerves and sinuses and maximising bone support. Faster Procedure, Shorter Recovery Because the surgery is so precise, it is often faster, requires fewer incisions, and causes less swelling, discomfort, and healing time.

Ensures optimal angulation and positioning Minimizes surgical time and trauma Allows us to design a custom surgical guide for flawless precision CAD/CAM Technology (Computer-Aided Design & Manufacturing) This tech designs and fabricates your crown, bridge, or full arch restoration with incredible accuracy.

Ridge Split / Expansion Why it ’ s needed: If your jawbone is too narrow to place an implant, we may gently split and expand the bone ridge, making room for implant placement—sometimes done simultaneously. Every Step, Carefully Planned and Explained Not everyone will need these procedures—but if you do, we’ll explain them clearly, plan them carefully, and carry them out with the latest technology and a gentle approach. Our promise is to never over-treat.

Implant Exposure Surgery (Second-Stage Surgery) Why it ’ s done: In some cases, after the implant is placed and healed under the gum, a minor second procedure is performed to expose the implant and attach the healing cap before fitting the crown. Is it complex. Not at all. It’s a quick and straightforward outpatient procedure under local anaesthesia.

Benefits: Improved aesthetics, especially in the front teeth Healthier gums around your implant Reduced risk of long-term complications Guided Bone Regeneration (GBR) Why it ’ s needed: Sometimes, we place a membrane over the grafted area to guide bone and tissue growth, especially if both are deficient. How it helps: This technique encourages your body to regrow bone while protecting the implant site. It’s a common part of modern implantology.

Sinus Lift or Sinus Augmentation Why it ’ s needed: If the upper back jaw (near the molars) doesn’t have enough bone height, and the sinuses are too close to the implant site, we perform a sinus lift. What happens: We gently lift the sinus membrane and add bone graft material to create enough space and support for an implant. Is it painful. Not at all. This is a standard and safe procedure, done under local anaesthesia or light sedation.

To ensure that, some patients may require supportive surgical procedures before or during implant placement. These minor surgeries help create a strong, stable foundation—especially if there’s bone loss, gum recession, or structural issues. Let’s walk you through the most common ones: Tooth Extraction Why it ’ s done: If your natural tooth is damaged beyond repair, it must be removed before placing an implant.

Soft Tissue Grafting (Gum Grafts) Why it ’ s needed: Some patients have thin or receding gums, which can expose the implant or affect aesthetics. Soft tissue grafting helps strengthen the gum area and creates a more natural-looking gum line.

Types of bone grafts: Minor socket preservation during extraction Sinus lift (for upper back teeth, see below) Ridge augmentation (to widen or heighten the jaw ridge) Block grafts (for larger bone defects) Materials used: We use biocompatible grafting material, either synthetic, donor-derived, or from your own bone, chosen based on your comfort and goals.

If you don’t need it, we won’t recommend it. But if you do—we’ll ensure it’s done with precision and comfort. Why Cleaning and Infection Control Matter Before Dental Implant Placement Placing a dental implant is like building a house—you need a strong, clean foundation before construction begins. That’s why oral health and infection control before implant surgery are non-negotiable.

How we do it: We use gentle, minimally invasive techniques to ensure a pain-free extraction with quick healing. In some cases, we can place the implant on the same day as the extraction. Bone Grafting or Bone Augmentation Why it ’ s needed: When a tooth has been missing for a while, the jawbone may shrink or weaken. Implants need solid bone to anchor into, so bone grafting helps rebuild what’s been lost.

Why Cleaning and Infection Control Matter During Dental Implant Placement When you undergo dental implant surgery at Pearl Dental Clinic Dubai, you’re not just getting advanced technology and expert hands—you’re getting a clinic that follows strict surgical hygiene protocols at every step. Even the most perfectly crafted veneers can wear down, chip, or cause discomfort if your bite isn’t balanced or if your jaw is under constant pressure. Why Your Bite Matters (Occlusion Check) Occlusion is how your upper and lower teeth come together when you chew or rest your jaw.

If your bite is off even slightly it can: Put excess pressure on certain veneers Lead to chipping, cracking, or early veneer failure Cause uneven wear and jaw tension over time Understanding TMJ (Temporomandibular Joint) The TMJ is the small, powerful joint that controls your jaw movement.

How Missing Teeth Affect Veneer Success When one or more teeth are missing, the neighboring teeth can shift, and the opposing teeth can over-erupt, throwing the entire bite out of alignment. This puts excessive pressure on certain areas including your veneers. So before placing veneers, we assess the entire dental system , not just the visible teeth.

Steps We Take to Correct and Stabilize Your Bite Digital Bite Scan & Occlusion Analysis We start with a precise 3D scan to understand your bite dynamics and identify any imbalances. At Pearl Dental Clinic Dubai, we pay close attention to something many clinics overlook: the margins of your veneers. These are the ultra-fine edges where your veneer meets your natural tooth. It might sound like a tiny detail but it can make a huge difference in both aesthetics and oral health. What Are Veneer Margins. The margin is the seam where the veneer ends and the natural tooth begins.

Improved Visibility & Precision With the gums and surrounding tissue safely retracted, we get unobstructed access to the veneer margins, allowing for accurate placement and polish. Better Infection Control Rubber dams minimize exposure to saliva and bacteria , helping maintain sterility especially important during bonding and preparation. Patient Safety & Comfort They prevent materials or tiny instruments from being swallowed or inhaled and keep the procedure more comfortable for many patients.

What Is a Rubber Dam. A rubber dam is a thin, flexible sheet (usually latex or latex-free) that’s placed around your tooth or teeth to keep the area dry, clean, and protected during treatment. Why We Use It for Veneers: Moisture Control = Stronger Bonding For veneers to adhere perfectly, the tooth surface must stay completely dry and clean. Saliva contamination can weaken the bond. Rubber dams keep the area sealed and moisture-free during cementation.

Our Infection Control Protocols Include: Medical-Grade Sterilization All instruments are sterilized using Class B autoclaves (hospital standard), and we use sealed pouches to maintain sterility until your treatment begins. Single-Use Disposables Wherever possible, we use disposable items gloves, masks, suction tips, and barriers discarded after every patient. Surface Disinfection Treatment areas are thoroughly disinfected before and after each procedure using hospital-grade solutions.

When Do We Use Rubber Dams.
